cards.text:
Herbs: Herbs are small plants with green, soft stems.
Example: Wheat, paddy, cabbage, grass, coriander, etc.

Herbs:
  • A herb is a non-woody plant that has green and tender stems with few branches and is usually short.
  • The steams are often juicy or fleshy.
  • They grow to a height of about 1-3 feet.

Shrubs: Shrubs are taller than herbs and have stems branching out from the base.
For example, lemon, coriander, Tulsi, rose, etc.

Shrubs:
  • The stems of shrubs are hard not but thick.
  • A common place where shrubs are grown is known as a shrubbery.
  • These are busy and medium-sized plants.
  • Their branches start from just above the ground.

Tree: They are tall plants with a clear hard and woody stem.
Examples are Mango, eucalyptus, teak, sal, neem, and peepal.

Tree:

  • The shape, structure, and height of stems are different in different trees.
  • A tree is a woody plant that has many branches on a single stem.
  • Trees benefit us by preventing soil erosion, maintaining the carbon dioxide content in the air and in building huts, and making furniture.
  • They generally grow for many years.

Creepers: Plants with a weak stems that cannot stand upright and spread on the ground are called creepers.
Creepers have a tendency to develop new plants on their own.
Example: Pumpkin, watermelon, sweet potato, etc.

Climbers: Plants with a weak stem that needs support is called climber.
Examples: Grapevine, money-plant, cucumber, bean, etc.

Climbers:
  • Climbers are plants that need support from other structures to grow and spread.
  • Climbers can either move horizontally or vertically unlike creepers that move only horizontally.
  • Climbers attract insects and reptiles because of their brightly coloured flowers and fragrance
Flowering Plants: Plants that bear flowers are called flowering plants. Their bodies are divided into roots, stems, and leaves and bear flowers and fruits.

• Stem usually grows above the ground.
  • The stem makes the main structural framework of the plant.
  • The stem bears leaves, branches, buds, flowers, and fruits.

• The stem of tree is known as the trunk.
  • Most of the trunk are covered with a tough outer layer called bark. Bark protect the inner part of the tree.

• The small outgrowths on the stem of a plant are called buds.
  • Buds are of two types terminal and axillary.
  • The point from where branches or leaves grow is called a node. The portion of a stem between two consecutive nodes is called the internodes.

The main functions of the Stem:

  1. The stem gives structural support to the plant.
  2. It transport water and nutrients.
  3. It bears branches, leaves, flowers, and fruits.
  4. Stem gets modified for food storage in some plants. E.g. potato, turmeric, etc.
Potato, yams, ginger, onion, etc. though present in the soil, are actually stem and store food within them.

• The leaf is a thin, flat and green structure which arises from the nodes of the stem.
  • The green colour of leaves is due to the presence of chlorophyll. chlorophyll is a green-coloured pigment.

• A leaf grows on the stem at a node.
  • The flat green portion of the leaf is called the leaf blade or lamina.
  • The leaf is attached to the stem by a short stalk called a petiole.
  • The petiole continues in the leaf as a thick midrib in the center.
  • The veins support water, minerals, and food.

Venation: Arrangement of veins in a leaf is called venation. There are two types of venation
  1. Reticulate venation
  2. Parallel venation

1. Reticulate venation: When veins make a network-like structure, it is called reticulate venation. E.g. banyan, mango, jackfruit, etc.
  2. Parallel venation: When the veins run parallel to each other, it is called parallel venation. E.g. paddy, wheat, grass, banana, etc.
Stomata: There are many small openings on the lower surface of a leaf. These are called stomata. Stomata allow gases to enter or exit the leaf.

Function of leaves 
  • Transpiration: It is the process of evaporation of water from the surface of leaves.
  • Photosynthesis: Green leaves synthesize food with the help of sunlight, air, and water by a process called photosynthesis.
  • Breathing: Stomata in leaves also facilitate breathing by leaves.

In the presence of sunlight, green leaves mix together water, minerals and carbon dioxide to make food. This process of making food by green plants is called photosynthesis.
Carbon dioxide + Water {tex}\xrightarrow[{Sunlight}]{Chlorophyll}{/tex} Glucose + Oxygen

Transpiration help plants in the following ways:
It helps in cooling the leaves, just as the loss of water during sweating helps us to keep cool.
When leaves lose water during transpiration, more water and minerals are pulled upwards from the roots to make up for the lost water. Thus leaves help in the conduction of water and minerals.

• The part of the plant that grows under the ground is called root.
  • Roots are of two types - tap root and fibrous root.

Tap root: A primary root that grows more or less straight down into the soil, and is tapered towards the end. This main root is called the tap root.
There are many smaller roots that branch out from the tap root called lateral roots. Examples of plants with tap roots are hibiscus, carrot, turnip and sunflower.
Fibrous roots: Many lateral roots that start from the base of the stem are called fibrous roots. Examples of plants with fibrous roots are banana, grass, and onion.
Root hair: The tiny thread-like structures that come over the roots are the root hair. The tip of the root is called the root cap.

The main functions of the roots:

  1. Absorb water and nutrients from the soil
  2. They store food like sugars and carbohydrates
  3. The roots hold the plant firmly in the soil, thereby serving as an anchor to the plant.
The flower is the reproductive part of a plant.

• The green leaf-like parts surrounding the bud are called the sepals.
  • They protect the bud before it blooms into a flower.
  • The sepals are collectively called the calyx.

• The brightly coloured parts of a flower are called the petals.
  • It helps plants attract insects and birds for pollination.
The male part of a flower is called the stamen, and the female part is called the pistil.
The sepals are collectively called the calyx.
The petals are collectively called the corolla.
In some flowers, the petals and sepals combine to form a round shape, which is called the petal.
Fertilization: The male and female parts of a flower are involved in the formation of fruit in a process called fertilization.

The male reproductive part of a flower: The stamen is the male reproductive part of a flower.

  1. The small tube with a little bulge at the end of a stamen is called the filament.
  2. The filament has a yellow sac called the anther.
  3. This yellow sac contains pollen grains, which contain male reproductive cells.

The female reproductive part of a flower: The pistil consists of three parts - the stigma, the style and the ovary.
  1. The upper end of the pistil is called the stigma, where pollen grains get deposited and enter the pistil.
  2. The narrow tubular part is called the style, which connects the stigma to the lower part of the pistil.
  3. The lower bulgy part of the pistil is called the ovary. It contains ovules.
  4. The female reproductive cells are ovules

Male reproductive organs (stamen):

  • Anther: produces pollen grains
  • Filament: supports anther above female reproductive organs

Female reproductive organs (carpel):

  • Stigma: sticky landing site for pollen grains
  • Style: tube that leads down to ovary 
  • Ovary: contains ovules that develop into seeds
Pedicel: The stalk that joins a flower to the plant is called the pedicel.
Pollination: For a flower to develop into a fruit and form seeds, pollen grains must be transferred from its anthers to the stigma.
